Create ad for Moon's Creed — facts: indie card-battler with run-based roguelike structure, mod support, draws on two well-known roguelike references.

I Combined Two of the Best Roguelikes to Create a Moddable Deck-Building Game => Moon's Creed

Lead with “I” to make the claim feel builder-led, not corporate. Make the creator the entry point; that signals a personal dev story and gives the sentence proof energy before the product name appears. Use “Combined” as the first action verb because it carries construction, experimentation, and novelty in one word. It tells the reader this is not just another genre entry; it is a deliberate fusion.

Put “Two of the Best Roguelikes” immediately after the verb to borrow category authority fast. “Two” is concrete; it creates curiosity because the reader wants to know which references are being merged. “Best” raises the stakes without naming competitors, letting fans fill in their own high-status examples. “Roguelikes” anchors the audience instantly: run-based players, deck-builder fans, and indie discovery browsers know the shelf this belongs on.

Keep “to Create” in the middle because it turns the first half from a comparison into a maker’s promise. The phrase shows intent and output: this is not commentary about influences; it resulted in a playable thing. Follow with “a Moddable Deck-Building Game” because the payoff stacks the most commercially useful features in a clean product descriptor. “Moddable” comes first, before “Deck-Building,” because it differentiates the game inside a crowded deck-builder market. “Deck-Building” names the core loop. “Game” closes the descriptor simply, avoiding over-explaining mechanics.

Use title case across the whole line to make it read like a video title or creator post rather than polished brand copy. That matches the likely discovery environment for an indie game: feed-native, maker-native, and curiosity-driven. Preserve the “=>” arrow because it functions like a reveal cut. The left side is the premise; the right side is the answer. The symbol is informal and technical, which fits a moddable game and a dev-forward claim. It also compresses the transition harder than a period or colon would.

End with “Moon's Creed” after the arrow, not at the beginning. Make the concept earn attention first, then attach the name to the curiosity. The product title lands as the reveal, so readers who are pulled in by the fusion claim have a clear label to remember. Keep the whole ad to one sentence because the strength is the single high-concept pitch: creator action, category prestige, differentiating feature, product reveal. No extra line dilutes the snap.

the sentence being only one line is part of why it works. there’s no second thought, no distracting benefit stack, no forced urgency. it reads like a clean catalog-style ad, which is exactly the kind of confidence you want with engine parts. the period at the end gives it a finished, matter-of-fact feel — not shouty, not hypey, just specific and ready to buy.

hey — can you help me write a short ad for CompiledSanity? it’s a personal finance spreadsheet that lets people track their net worth and finances. it’s built to be smart and secure, and it’s gotten a lot of attention online. mostly just need the core ad copy.

yeah — i’d keep it tight and run it like this:

Try out the #1 Personal Finance Spreadsheet 📊 Track your Net Worth and grow your finances with this smart, secure & internet famous Finance tool.

the move here is that it doesn’t over-explain the product. it opens with “Try out,” which feels low-friction — not “buy,” not “commit,” not “switch your whole financial life over.” it sounds like something the reader can sample, which matters because spreadsheets can feel intimidating or like work. “Try out” makes the ask feel easy.

“the #1 Personal Finance Spreadsheet” is doing the authority work immediately. the “#1” is visually compact and punchy, and because it comes before the category, the reader understands the positioning before they even get to the product function. capitalizing “Personal Finance Spreadsheet” also makes the category feel like a proper, established thing rather than just a generic file.

the 📊 emoji is small but useful. it breaks up what could otherwise be a dry finance sentence, and it gives the ad a quick visual cue for tracking, charts, numbers, and progress. it also keeps the tone from feeling too corporate — personal finance can feel serious, but the emoji makes the tool feel approachable.

then the second half gets practical fast: “Track your Net Worth and grow your finances.” that sequence is smart because it starts with the concrete action — “Track” — then moves to the desired outcome — “grow.” “Net Worth” being capitalized makes that phrase stand out as the central thing the spreadsheet helps with. it’s not just budgeting or expense logging; it’s pointing at the bigger personal-finance scoreboard.

“grow your finances” is broad enough to cover different reader goals: saving more, investing better, paying down debt, organizing accounts, building wealth. it doesn’t trap the product in one narrow use case.

the final phrase, “with this smart, secure & internet famous Finance tool,” stacks reassurance and social proof. “smart” suggests the spreadsheet is more than a blank template. “secure” handles the obvious trust concern around money. the ampersand in “secure & internet famous” keeps the rhythm quick and modern, almost social-platform-native. and “internet famous” gives it a bit of cultural proof — not just useful, but already discovered and talked about.

also, keeping everything in one sentence helps the ad feel fast. there’s no slow setup, no extra explanation, no feature list. it moves from invitation, to category leadership, to visual cue, to core benefit, to trust/social proof in a single clean run.

Create ad for DITOGAMES — browser game, free access, runs without installing software, controlled with a standard computer mouse.

If you have a mouse, you have to play this game.

Play for free. No Installation.

Keep the hook as one plain conditional sentence. “If you have a mouse” makes the entry requirement feel absurdly low: not a device list, not a spec, just the object already in the reader’s hand. The comma creates a quick hinge; the first half qualifies almost everyone, then “you have to play this game” converts that qualification into an obligation. “Have to” is stronger than “can” or “should”; it makes the game feel culturally unavoidable without explaining genre, mechanics, or features. “This game” stays deliberately unspecific, forcing attention onto the claim and keeping curiosity open.

Preserve the blank line. It separates the big, conversational dare from the practical reassurance, so the reader first feels pulled in, then gets objections removed. “Play for free.” is short, complete, and final; the period makes the benefit feel settled, not promotional. “No Installation.” answers the next friction point in two words. The capital “Installation” gives the term weight, like a product requirement being crossed out, and the period keeps it crisp. Sequence matters: possession of a mouse → social/behavioral push to play → free access → zero setup. The copy wins by shrinking the decision until playing feels easier than ignoring it.

Draft ad for Vance Ginn — facts: covers President-elect Trump tariff proposals, Texas government spending, taxpayer protection, economic freedom, and Texas’s new speaker.

Dive into the critical issues shaping economic policy, from President-elect Trump’s sweeping tariff proposals to the challenges Texas faces under rising government spending. 

Now is the time for bold reforms to protect taxpayers and ensure economic freedom thrives.

Trump’s Tariff Threats and Texas’s New Speaker

Lead with “Dive” to make the reader enter the topic instead of merely read about it. The first sentence builds scope fast: “critical issues shaping economic policy” names importance before the examples arrive, so the ad feels consequential from the first line. The comma after “policy” creates a clean pivot into specifics, and the “from…to…” construction gives the piece range: national trade policy on one end, Texas fiscal pressure on the other. “President-elect Trump’s sweeping tariff proposals” uses “sweeping” to signal scale and disruption without overexplaining. “The challenges Texas faces” keeps the state-level issue concrete, while “under rising government spending” frames the pressure as ongoing and intensifying.

The blank line after the first sentence matters. It lets the issue setup stand alone, then gives the second paragraph room to shift from diagnosis to action. “Now is the time” creates urgency without an exclamation point. “Bold reforms” gives the solution a muscular, policy-forward feel. The paired verbs “protect” and “ensure” make the promise feel active: one defensive, one constructive. “Taxpayers” identifies who is at stake in plain language. “Economic freedom thrives” ends the body on an aspirational phrase, moving beyond complaint into a positive governing principle.

The final line works as the title and the closing anchor. “Trump’s Tariff Threats” is punchy because of the hard T sounds and the word “Threats,” which raises stakes without needing a longer explanation. “Texas’s New Speaker” adds a second political hook and makes the piece feel timely. The title’s structure mirrors the body’s national-to-state movement: Trump first, Texas second. No extra CTA weakens it; the ad closes on the subject itself, trusting the tension in the title to pull the click.
You are an ad copywriter. When a user describes a product or campaign, you write ad copy and a short rationale explaining why the execution works.

Product: Grove Finance, an onchain finance project focused on allocating capital into tokenized credit opportunities.
Audience: Crypto-native investors, DeFi participants, and people following institutional credit market activity onchain.
Goal: Create a concise social ad that encourages people to follow Grove.
Facts: More than $2B has already been allocated. Rewards are directed back into the Sky Ecosystem. The ad should communicate that Grove is connected to the evolution of credit markets.

Recommended copy:

Grove deploys capital into tokenized credit strategies onchain.

Over $2B already allocated and counting, with rewards routing back into the Sky Ecosystem.

Follow Grove to see how credit markets are evolving.

Follow Grove

**Hook.** The opening sentence is direct and institutional: “Grove deploys capital into tokenized credit strategies onchain.” It begins with the brand name, which establishes immediate ownership of the action. The verb “deploys” is precise and finance-native; it signals active capital allocation rather than vague participation. “Capital” gives the line weight, while “tokenized credit strategies” defines the category without overexplaining it. Ending with “onchain” places the entire proposition in the crypto-native environment in a single word, avoiding a slower phrase such as “on the blockchain.”

**Structure.** The ad moves in three clean steps: what Grove does, proof of scale, and why to follow. The first line defines the business. The second line substantiates it with “Over $2B already allocated and counting,” then connects that activity to the Sky Ecosystem. The third line turns the information into a reason to subscribe: “Follow Grove to see how credit markets are evolving.” This sequence works because the reader is not asked to follow before being given a concrete reason to care.

**Proof point.** “Over $2B already allocated and counting” is the credibility anchor. “Over” creates scale without requiring excessive precision. “Already” implies momentum and maturity. “And counting” adds forward motion, making the number feel current rather than static. The comma after “counting” creates a clean pivot into the ecosystem benefit, so the proof point does not sit isolated; it becomes part of a broader value loop.

**Ecosystem connection.** “With rewards routing back into the Sky Ecosystem” is efficient because it explains where value flows without adding a technical diagram. “Routing back” suggests a deliberate mechanism, not an incidental benefit. The capitalization of “Sky Ecosystem” treats it as a proper destination and reinforces that Grove’s activity is connected to a larger network rather than operating as a standalone financial product.

**Call to follow.** The sentence “Follow Grove to see how credit markets are evolving.” is stronger than a generic follow request because it gives the follow a content promise. The phrase “credit markets are evolving” broadens the ad beyond one product update; it positions Grove as a lens on a market transition. The final standalone “Follow Grove” then functions as the clean action button. Repeating “Follow Grove” once in the body and once as the CTA reinforces the desired behavior without feeling excessive, because the first instance explains the reason and the second isolates the action.

**Line breaks and pacing.** Each sentence is separated by a blank line, giving the ad a measured, high-confidence rhythm. The spacing prevents the technical terms from feeling dense. The final CTA on its own line is visually decisive and easy to act on. The ad does not use hype punctuation, emojis, or exaggerated claims; that restraint fits a finance audience that is likely to respond better to specificity, scale, and credible market positioning.

Create ad for Range — stablecoin transfer explorer with coverage for USDC, USDT, USD1, RLUSD, 100+ networks, 20 bridges, entity labeling, and blacklist data.

Every stablecoin. Every chain. One explorer.

Track USDC, USDT, USD1, RLUSD and more, across 100+ chains and 20 bridges - with full entity + blacklist visibility.

Learn more 👇

Track every stablecoin transfer

The first line wins by using three clipped fragments instead of one explanatory sentence. “Every stablecoin.” sets maximum scope immediately. “Every chain.” repeats the same two-word rhythm, making coverage feel complete rather than partial. “One explorer.” breaks the pattern at the exact right moment: after two broad promises, it collapses the complexity into a single product. The period after each fragment matters; each claim lands as final, not conversational.

The sequence moves from big category promise to concrete proof. After “Every stablecoin. Every chain. One explorer.” the next line names assets: “USDC, USDT, USD1, RLUSD and more.” That list makes the claim verifiable. It starts with the most recognized tickers, then adds newer/specific ones, then uses “and more” to preserve breadth without bloating the sentence. The comma-separated list creates speed and density, matching a crypto-native reader’s expectation for ticker-heavy information.

“across 100+ chains and 20 bridges” adds scale with numbers, not adjectives. “100+” is stronger than “many” because it signals infrastructure coverage. “20 bridges” matters because stablecoin movement is cross-chain, not just chain-by-chain; including bridges tells the reader the explorer follows the paths where transfers actually happen. The hyphen before “with full entity + blacklist visibility” creates a second payoff inside the same sentence: first breadth, then intelligence.

“full entity + blacklist visibility” is compact and technical. “full” removes the worry that the product only shows raw transfers. “entity” implies attribution and context. “blacklist” adds risk, compliance, and monitoring relevance. The plus sign in “entity + blacklist” is doing useful work: it feels native to dashboards and data products, saves space, and visually binds the two capabilities as a combined layer.

The line breaks make the ad scannable. The opener stands alone as the positioning. The middle paragraph carries the feature proof. “Learn more 👇” gets its own line so the action is unmistakable. The pointing emoji is simple but functional; it directs attention downward without adding more copy. The final line, “Track every stablecoin transfer,” acts like a CTA button and repeats the core promise with the verb “Track.” Ending on “transfer” narrows the broad stablecoin/category claim into the actual use case, so the reader knows exactly what happens after the click.

Need ad for New Economy Canada — facts: Canadian initiative covering renewables, mineral supply chains, high-tech production, economic modernization.

From clean energy to critical minerals, and advanced manufacturing, Canada’s building the industries that power a cleaner, stronger, more competitive economy. Discover how we’re helping shape the new economy.

Canada’s New Economy

Lead with “From clean energy to critical minerals, and advanced manufacturing” because the range does immediate positioning work. “From” opens a sweep, not a list; it makes the initiative feel national-scale before the reader reaches the subject. “clean energy” starts with the most familiar public-good category, “critical minerals” adds urgency and strategic relevance, and “advanced manufacturing” closes on jobs, production, and industrial capacity. The comma before “and advanced manufacturing” slows the list just enough to give the third item weight instead of making it feel like an afterthought.

Use “Canada’s building” instead of a more formal construction because the contraction gives the line motion and confidence. It makes the country feel active now, not planning eventually. “the industries” is concrete and plural, which keeps the claim from floating as vague economic optimism. “that power” carries a double meaning: literal energy and broader economic force. That verb links the first category, “clean energy,” to the larger argument without explaining it.

Stack “a cleaner, stronger, more competitive economy” in that order. “cleaner” ties back to energy and climate, “stronger” broadens into resilience and prosperity, and “more competitive” lands on the business and global-economy case. The repeated comma cadence makes the benefits feel cumulative. “more competitive” is longer than the first two adjectives, so the sentence finishes with the most institutional, policy-facing payoff.

Move to “Discover how we’re helping shape the new economy” as a soft but clear action. “Discover” asks for engagement without sounding like a hard sell. “how” promises substance behind the claim. “we’re helping” keeps the organization credible by avoiding overclaiming; it participates in shaping the future rather than pretending to own it alone. “shape” is the right verb because it suggests intentional influence, not passive observation. “the new economy” repeats the campaign idea in plain language before the final lockup.

End with the standalone line “Canada’s New Economy” because the ad needs a clean memory anchor after the explanatory sentence. The line break isolates the name, turning it into a platform rather than another sentence. The possessive “Canada’s” makes the idea national and shared, while “New Economy” gives the campaign a concise, ownable frame. The structure works because it moves from sectors, to national action, to economic benefit, to invitation, to brand idea — each step tightening the reader’s understanding without adding clutter.

hey, quick one for you — we’ve got The Rundown AI, a free email newsletter. it sends short 5-minute guides about AI tools and how to use them. it has over 500,000 readers. can you write a simple paid social ad for it?

yep — i’d keep it super direct and make the value feel obvious fast:

Here are the top AI tools of 2024.

We write 5-minute guides on how to use them in our free newsletter.

Join 500,000+ readers and find out how the early adopters are (actually) using AI:

➡️ Click to Join Free

the move here is that it doesn’t try to sound clever before it’s useful. “Here are the top AI tools of 2024.” is a clean, immediate hook because it gives the reader a concrete promise right away: there are tools, they’re current, and someone has done the sorting for them. “top” does a lot of work because it implies curation, not just information. “of 2024” makes the ad feel timely, which matters a ton in AI because people assume anything even a few months old might already be outdated.

the second line turns that curiosity into a product explanation without overexplaining it: “We write 5-minute guides on how to use them in our free newsletter.” the phrase “5-minute guides” lowers the effort barrier. it tells the reader this isn’t a giant course, a dense report, or some technical rabbit hole. “on how to use them” is also important because it shifts from discovery to application. the ad isn’t just saying “learn about AI tools”; it’s saying you’ll know what to do with them. and “free newsletter” removes the main friction right there in the same sentence, before the reader has time to wonder what the catch is.

then the third line adds proof and a sharper reason to care: “Join 500,000+ readers and find out how the early adopters are (actually) using AI:” the number gives the offer social proof without needing hype words. “500,000+” feels big and established, while the plus sign makes it feel like it’s still growing. “early adopters” positions the reader near the front edge of the shift, which is exactly the status people want around AI. the parenthetical “(actually)” is the best little piece of skepticism in the ad. it quietly acknowledges that there’s a lot of AI noise, vague claims, and fake expertise out there. that one word makes the promise feel more practical and grounded.

the colon at the end of that line is doing nice conversion work too. it sets up the CTA as the natural next step, not a separate command. the reader gets the promise, the proof, the practical angle, and then the colon basically says: if you want that, do this.

the final line, “➡️ Click to Join Free,” is simple and very clickable. the arrow emoji pulls the eye down to the action, especially in a feed where people are scanning. “Click” is direct, “Join” makes it feel like becoming part of something rather than just subscribing, and “Free” repeats the zero-risk point at the exact moment of action. ending on “Free” is stronger than burying it earlier because it leaves the reader with the lowest-friction thought possible.

the structure is also really tight: current curiosity, quick explanation, social proof plus practical payoff, then CTA. each line earns the next one. no long setup, no brand mythology, no clever metaphor — just “this is useful, it’s fast, lots of people already read it, and you can get it free.”

hey, quick one for you

i’m trying to write a short ad for Blue Lagoon Resort Fiji. there’s a promo where if someone books a 7-night stay for travel between October and March 2025, we pay for their round-trip Fiji Airways flights. want something simple for the offer.

i’d run it exactly like this:

the timing is tucked right into the first line: “October thru March 2025.” that keeps the offer feeling concrete without turning the opening into terms and conditions. “thru” also feels more casual and ad-like than “through,” which helps the line move quickly. then the payoff lands at the end: “and fly for free.” putting that last gives the sentence a little snap — destination first, dates second, reward last. the period after it also helps it feel complete and confident, not hypey.

the line break is important too. the first line sells the big promise; the second line explains the mechanism. that separation keeps the ad from feeling cluttered. someone can understand the whole offer from the first sentence, then the second sentence answers the natural follow-up: “what do I have to do?”

“Stay 7 nights,” is super efficient. it starts the second line with the requirement, and the comma after it creates a clean if/then rhythm without actually saying “if.” then “we will cover” feels reassuring because it’s active and personal — the resort is taking responsibility for the airfare, not hiding behind vague promo language. “your round trip airfares” adds the practical detail people need to believe the offer, and ending on “Fiji Airways” gives it specificity and credibility. it makes the free-flight claim feel tied to a real airline, not just a generic discount.
You are an ad copywriter. When a user describes a product or campaign, you write ad copy and a short rationale explaining why the execution works.

Product: Ripple educational content about stablecoins for companies evaluating blockchain-based payment and settlement options.
Audience: Business leaders, finance teams, treasury teams, and payments decision-makers.
Goal: Create concise ad copy that drives interest in the educational content.
Facts: Stablecoins are digital assets typically tied to fiat currencies. Businesses may use them to understand faster settlement, cross-border value movement, liquidity, and digital-asset payment infrastructure. The asset is educational rather than a direct product checkout.

Recommended execution:

Learn how stablecoins work and why they are becoming increasingly important for your business.

**Hook.** The line opens with “Learn,” which is a direct but low-friction imperative. It does not pressure the reader to buy, demo, switch providers, or commit budget. That matters because the offer is educational and the audience is likely still evaluating the category. “Learn” correctly frames the next step as knowledge acquisition, making the ad feel useful rather than promotional.

**Structure.** The sentence is built around a clean two-part promise: “how stablecoins work” and “why they are becoming increasingly important for your business.” The first half addresses basic comprehension; the second half addresses relevance and urgency. That sequence is important. It begins with the foundational question a cautious business reader may have, then moves into the strategic reason to care. The “and” links the two benefits without making the sentence feel overloaded.

**Specific wording.** “Stablecoins” appears early and unadorned, which keeps the subject clear. The phrase “how stablecoins work” is plain and practical; “work” is less intimidating than technical alternatives such as “operate,” “function,” or “are implemented.” It signals that the content will make the topic understandable. “Why” then shifts the reader from mechanics to business context, which broadens the appeal beyond technical teams to finance and executive stakeholders.

**Business relevance.** The phrase “becoming increasingly important” creates momentum without resorting to hype. “Becoming” suggests an active market shift, and “increasingly” implies that the importance is growing over time. Together, they create urgency while remaining credible. The copy does not claim that stablecoins are already mandatory or transformative for every company; it positions them as a trend worth understanding now.

**Audience fit.** The closing phrase “for your business” is doing substantial work. Without it, the line would read like a general educational prompt. With it, the ad becomes directly relevant to a commercial reader. “Your” personalizes the value, and “business” frames stablecoins as an operational and strategic topic rather than a purely crypto-native or consumer topic.

**Pacing and punctuation.** The sentence is a single, uninterrupted line with no commas, dashes, or parentheticals. That gives it a smooth, accessible cadence. The final period reinforces the professional tone; there is no exclamation point, no sensationalism, and no forced excitement. The restraint supports credibility, which is especially valuable for financial-technology messaging.

**Why it works for Ripple.** The copy lets the category lead. It does not force the brand into the sentence, which keeps the educational offer from feeling self-serving. For a company associated with blockchain payments and digital-asset infrastructure, this is a strong choice: the ad earns attention by promising clarity on a timely business topic, then allows the brand’s authority to sit behind that promise.
You are an ad copywriter. When a user describes a product or campaign, you write ad copy and a short rationale explaining why the execution works.

hey, quick one for you

can you help write a short ad for The Free Press? it’s for a personal essay about marriage — the writer talks about going through a really hard stretch with her spouse, hearing from couples who have been together a long time, and rethinking what a lasting relationship actually feels like from the inside.

sure — I’d keep it spare and let the emotional turn do the work:

"When I listen to people who have been married for decades, I hear the same thing over and over: ‘Back then, we didn’t know if we’d make it.’"

I Thought My Marriage Was Broken. Then I Stayed In It.

what makes this land is how little it tries to “sell” the piece. the opening line feels like overheard wisdom, not promo copy. starting with `"When I listen to people who have been married for decades` immediately gives the ad borrowed authority: these aren’t therapists, experts, or influencers — they’re people with time behind them. “for decades” is the proof point, and it does a lot of quiet work because it makes the reader assume these marriages have survived things.

the phrase `I hear the same thing over and over` is also doing more than it looks like. it creates a pattern before revealing the pattern. that repetition — “same thing” plus “over and over” — primes the reader for something universal, like the ad is about to say the private thought many people have had but don’t say out loud. then the colon before the quoted line gives the sentence a little pause and makes the next phrase feel like a reveal.

the quote inside the quote is the emotional center: `‘Back then, we didn’t know if we’d make it.’` the words are plain, almost conversational, which is why they hit. `Back then` implies distance and survival without spelling it out. `we didn’t know` is vulnerable, not dramatic. and `if we’d make it` is the exact kind of language people use when they’re describing a relationship from inside the uncertainty. it avoids tidy marriage-advice language and instead names the fear directly.

the line break is crucial. the first paragraph gives us the collective lesson from long-married people, then the blank space lets it sit. after that, the title arrives as the personal application: `I Thought My Marriage Was Broken. Then I Stayed In It.` that sequence makes the ad feel like a small story in two beats. first: the outside perspective. second: the writer’s own marriage.

the title itself is built on a sharp contrast. `I Thought My Marriage Was Broken.` is blunt and final. the period after “Broken” matters because it makes the thought feel closed, like a verdict. then `Then I Stayed In It.` reopens the story. that second sentence is simple, but it creates tension because “stayed” is not automatically framed as romantic or triumphant — it’s complicated. the reader wants to know whether staying was wise, painful, transformative, or all of the above.

also, the ad never says “save your marriage,” “relationship advice,” or “what I learned.” that restraint makes it feel more literary and more aligned with The Free Press: an essay with a point of view, not a self-help promise. the whole thing moves from universal observation to intimate confession, and that’s why it works as a click: it gives just enough truth to make the reader feel recognized, then withholds the actual resolution.
You are an ad copywriter. When a user describes a product or campaign, you write ad copy and a short rationale explaining why the execution works.

hey, can you help me write an ad for Fulton? they make insoles with custom molding technology. people use them as an alternative to custom orthotics, which can be expensive. there’s a customer named Linda F. who said she got rid of her custom orthotics after trying them. want something we can use as paid social copy.

sure — i’d keep it direct and let the customer quote do the heavy lifting:

"I got rid of my custom orthotics after trying these" - Linda F.

People are switching from expensive orthotics to Fulton insoles. Discover what all the hype is about.

Learn more about Fulton's custom molding technology

the move here is that the ad opens with a very specific customer outcome instead of a product claim. `"I got rid of my custom orthotics after trying these"` is strong because it sounds like an actual before-and-after moment, not brand language. “got rid of” is plainspoken and physical — you can picture someone literally stopping use of something they previously depended on. that’s more convincing than saying “better than orthotics,” because it implies the comparison through Linda’s behavior.

the phrase “custom orthotics” does a lot of work in the first line. it anchors the problem in a serious, high-consideration category, not just generic foot comfort. then “after trying these” keeps the barrier low. it doesn’t over-explain the product yet; it creates curiosity. the reader has to keep going to understand what “these” are.

the attribution `- Linda F.` matters too. the dash makes the quote feel testimonial-style, and the first name plus last initial gives just enough specificity to feel human without making the ad feel overly formal or clinical. it keeps the line casual and believable.

the blank line after the quote is important because it lets the testimonial land before the brand starts explaining. then the next sentence widens the story: `People are switching from expensive orthotics to Fulton insoles.` that sequence is clean — individual proof first, broader trend second. “People are switching” makes Linda feel like part of a bigger movement, while “from expensive orthotics to Fulton insoles” states the value proposition in one direct contrast. the word “expensive” is doing the economic framing without needing to name a price.

`Discover what all the hype is about.` shifts the energy from proof to curiosity. “hype” is casual and social, which helps the ad feel native to a feed instead of like medical-device copy. it suggests Fulton already has momentum, and the reader is being invited to catch up.

the final line, `Learn more about Fulton's custom molding technology`, gives the ad a credible landing point. after the testimonial and social-proof language, “custom molding technology” adds substance. it tells the reader there’s an actual mechanism behind the promise, not just a cheaper insert. the structure is doing a nice progression: customer proof, category switch, curiosity, then technology-backed CTA.
You are an ad copywriter. When a user describes a product or campaign, you write ad copy and a short rationale explaining why the execution works.

Product: A multi-part documentary series from TCN about President Trump’s political comeback and campaign operation
Audience: Conservative news viewers and politically engaged subscribers considering TCN membership
Goal: Drive membership signups by promoting first access to the series
Facts: Series title is Art of the Surge; subtitle is The Donald Trump Comeback; releases this summer; available to TCN members; includes access to President Trump and his campaign team; membership is required to view

Recommended copy:

Premiering this summer for TCN members: ART OF THE SURGE. Get rare unprecedented access to President Trump and his campaign team in this multi-part series. Join now to watch first »

Art of the Surge: The Donald Trump Comeback

**Hook.** The opening phrase, “Premiering this summer for TCN members,” immediately establishes timing, exclusivity, and eligibility in a single compact clause. “Premiering” gives the offer a launch-event quality rather than making it feel like ordinary library content. “This summer” creates a near-term release window without requiring an exact date. “For TCN members” places the access condition before the title, so the reader understands from the first sentence that membership is the gateway.

**Title treatment.** “ART OF THE SURGE” appears in all caps, which gives the series title weight and makes it function as the visual center of the first sentence. The colon before it is efficient: it turns the first clause into a formal announcement and lets the title arrive with emphasis. The phrase also echoes political comeback language through “SURGE,” making the title feel active, competitive, and campaign-oriented.

**Access promise.** “Get rare unprecedented access to President Trump and his campaign team” is the core value proposition. The sequence matters: “rare” signals scarcity, “unprecedented” raises the perceived importance, and “access” names the benefit plainly. Placing “President Trump” before “his campaign team” prioritizes the marquee figure while expanding the scope to include the operation around him. That combination suggests both personality-driven appeal and insider campaign coverage.

**Format clarity.** The phrase “in this multi-part series” gives the audience a concrete content format. It tells the reader this is not a one-off clip or single interview; it is a serialized product with multiple installments. That makes the membership offer feel more substantial because the implied value extends beyond one viewing session.

**Conversion line.** “Join now to watch first »” is concise and direct. “Join now” is the action. “To watch first” ties the action to the exclusive benefit rather than to membership in the abstract. The final “»” adds a visual forward motion, functioning like a lightweight button cue without needing a longer call-to-action line.

**Line break and final title.** The blank line before “Art of the Surge: The Donald Trump Comeback” separates the direct-response membership pitch from the program title lockup. The final line reads like a clean title card. It restates the name in title case rather than all caps, then adds the subtitle after the colon. “The Donald Trump Comeback” frames the series around a clear narrative arc, giving the audience an immediate reason to understand the documentary as a story of return, momentum, and political consequence.

**Audience fit.** For politically engaged TCN prospects, the ad avoids over-explaining the significance of the subject. It assumes recognition of President Trump and focuses instead on what the viewer gets: early member access, proximity to the candidate and team, and a multi-part account of the comeback. The copy is short, but every element carries a job: launch timing, exclusivity, title, access, format, action, and narrative framing.

hey — can you help write an ad for Reown?

it’s for AppKit SDK. it helps teams build Solana app experiences faster. includes 60+ wallet connections, social and email login, and a built-in onramp. devs can get live with about 20 lines of code. it’s made for Solana and meant to handle scale.

yep — i’d keep it tight and very dev-friendly:

Build smoother Solana UX, faster.

With Reown's AppKit SDK, you get:

✅ 60+ wallet connections
✅ Social and email login
✅ Built-in onramp

Just 20 lines of code to go live.

Built for Solana, designed for scale.

Build smoother Solana UX, faster.

the move here is that the ad opens with the outcome, not the product. “Build smoother Solana UX, faster.” is doing a lot in six words: “Build” speaks directly to developers, “smoother” names the quality improvement, “Solana UX” makes the ecosystem immediately specific, and “faster” adds the practical speed payoff. the comma before “faster” gives that last word a little punch, almost like a second benefit landing after the first one.

then the copy shifts cleanly into the product with “With Reown's AppKit SDK, you get:”. that line is simple and plain, which matters because the next section is a feature stack. the colon sets up the list without making the reader work, and “you get” feels concrete — not vague platform language, just here’s what’s included.

the three checkmark bullets are really efficient. “✅ 60+ wallet connections” leads with the biggest integration-heavy benefit, and the “60+” is the proof point that makes it feel substantial. “✅ Social and email login” comes next because it broadens the UX beyond crypto-native users. then “✅ Built-in onramp” closes the list with a conversion/access piece. the order is nice: connect wallets, let more users log in, help them get funds in. it quietly maps to the user journey.

the blank lines are also doing work. each section gets room to breathe: promise, product/features, implementation speed, positioning, repeated promise. that makes the ad feel quick to scan, which fits the developer audience. it doesn’t bury the important details in a paragraph.

“Just 20 lines of code to go live.” is the sharpest proof line. “Just” frames the implementation as low-friction, “20 lines of code” is specific enough to feel credible, and “to go live” turns the technical detail into an actual launch outcome. it’s not saying “easy integration” in generic terms — it shows the effort level.

“Built for Solana, designed for scale.” adds authority after the speed claim. the parallel structure makes it feel polished: “Built for” / “designed for.” it reassures the reader that this isn’t just a quick hack or a generic SDK; it’s ecosystem-specific and production-minded. again, the comma creates a balanced two-part line, which makes the sentence feel confident.

ending by repeating “Build smoother Solana UX, faster.” is a strong loop. after the reader has seen the feature proof and the “20 lines” implementation proof, the same opening line lands with more weight. it becomes the takeaway, not just the hook. the repetition also makes the ad feel more memorable, especially in a feed where people may only retain one line.

hey — can you help me write a paid social ad for The Free Press?

it’s an independent media/news publication. we publish essays and reporting from writers, including Freya India. this piece is about how people talk about ordinary personality traits through the language of mental health diagnoses now — things like shyness, forgetfulness, autism, ADHD.

basically want something that gets people to click and read the article.

ok, i’d keep this one really sharp and let the article’s own tension do the work:

"My generation is obsessed with treating every trait as a symptom of a disorder: You’re not shy, you’re autistic; you’re not forgetful, you’ve got ADHD," writes Freya India.

Nobody Has a Personality Anymore

the move here is that it opens mid-argument, not with context. “My generation” immediately gives the line a specific speaker and a generational stake — it doesn’t sound like a brand making a broad cultural claim from above. it sounds like someone inside the culture calling out her own cohort, which makes the critique feel sharper and less preachy.

“is obsessed” is doing a lot of work too. it’s not neutral language. it implies repetition, fixation, and excess, so the reader understands the article is going to challenge a pattern, not just describe a trend. then “every trait” widens the claim fast. in three words, the ad makes the issue feel big: not one diagnosis, not one social media habit, but an entire way of interpreting the self.

the colon after “disorder” is the hinge. everything before it is the thesis; everything after it is the proof. that punctuation makes the sentence feel clean and argumentative: claim, then examples. the examples are also perfectly sequenced because they use the same rhythm twice — “You’re not shy, you’re autistic; you’re not forgetful, you’ve got ADHD.” that parallel structure makes the idea instantly legible. the reader doesn’t have to work to understand the critique because the repeated “you’re not / you’re” pattern teaches the frame as they read.

the semicolon matters. it keeps the two examples connected inside one breath rather than splitting them into separate sentences. that makes the behavior feel like a recurring reflex: not shy becomes autistic; not forgetful becomes ADHD. the comma before “you’ve got ADHD” gives the line a conversational snap, almost like someone finishing a thought out loud. it feels natural, not clinical, which is important because the ad is criticizing clinical language overtaking ordinary personality language.

adding “writes Freya India” after the quote gives the claim authorship and credibility without slowing it down. it tells you this is an essay with a point of view, not just a floating provocation. and because her name comes after the quote, the reader encounters the argument first and the byline second — which is the right order for a feed ad. the hook has to land before the credential matters.

then the line break creates the payoff. after the quoted setup, “Nobody Has a Personality Anymore” lands like a title, but also like a punchline and a diagnosis of the diagnosis culture. it’s short, absolute, and a little unsettling. “Nobody” is intentionally sweeping; “Personality” is the ordinary human word set against all the disorder language above; “Anymore” adds cultural decline and urgency. the headline doesn’t explain the article — it crystallizes the feeling the quote just created.

the blank line between the quote and the title is important visually. it gives the reader a beat to absorb the examples, then the title reframes them into the larger idea. in a social feed, that pause makes the final line feel heavier. the ad works because it doesn’t over-explain The Free Press or the article. it gives one provocative excerpt, attributes it cleanly, then lets the title make the argument bigger.

Create ad for Slingshot News — facts: news outlet, @NewsSlingshot, published a first-reported story about Capitol Police Chief Thomas Manger and a scandal.
Push people to follow the account for future reporting.

🚨EXCLUSIVE: Slingshot News Takes Down Capitol Police Chief Thomas Manger After Bombshell Exclusive Report Reveals Major Scandal

Follow @NewsSlingshot for more exclusive reports!

Slingshot News Takes Down Capitol Police Chief With Exclusive Report

Start with the siren emoji because it turns the first line into an alert, not a passive update. Use “EXCLUSIVE:” in all caps before the outlet name so the reader understands the value before the story mechanics arrive: this is not aggregation, this is ownership. Put “Slingshot News” immediately after the colon to attach the scoop to the brand before any other entity can claim attention.

Make “Takes Down” the central verb pair. It is forceful, compressed, and outcome-driven. It tells the reader the report had consequences, not just information. Place “Capitol Police Chief Thomas Manger” after that verb so the target feels concrete and high-stakes. The full title plus full name gives the line institutional weight; it does not rely on vague “official” language.

Keep “After Bombshell Exclusive Report Reveals Major Scandal” as the causal engine. “After” creates sequence: report first, fallout second. “Bombshell” signals magnitude. Repeating “Exclusive” reinforces the brand’s access and separates the report from commodity news. “Reveals” makes the story feel uncovered rather than commented on. “Major Scandal” lands as the payoff phrase at the end of the sentence, where the reader’s eye naturally stops.

Insert a full blank line before “Follow @NewsSlingshot for more exclusive reports!” so the call to action has its own stage. Do not bury the handle inside the main claim. The handle is the action point, and the exclamation mark gives it urgency without adding another sentence. Use “more exclusive reports” to convert this single story into a promise of continuing value: following is framed as access to future scoops, not charity for a media page.

End with “Slingshot News Takes Down Capitol Police Chief With Exclusive Report” as a shorter restatement. This final line works like a link preview or secondary headline. It removes “Thomas Manger,” “Bombshell,” and “Major Scandal,” leaving the cleanest version of the claim: brand, consequence, authority figure, mechanism. The repetition is useful because social readers skim; the first line delivers drama, the middle line converts attention into a follow, and the last line restates the core news in a tighter package.
You are an ad copywriter. When a user describes a product or campaign, you write ad copy and a short rationale explaining why the execution works.
Need ad for STAT article — facts: analysis of Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s possible role in shaping FDA policy under Trump. Health and medicine news context.

RFK Jr. himself declared recently that the FDA’s “war on public health” was about to end.

What letting RFK Jr. ‘go wild’ might mean for Trump’s FDA

Lead with the most charged fact, then make the reader confront the consequence. “RFK Jr. himself declared” puts the subject on the record and makes the opening feel evidentiary, not speculative. “himself” does extra work: it signals direct attribution, raises stakes, and implies the quote is not secondhand chatter. “declared” is stronger than “said”; it carries authority, intent, and a hint of provocation, which fits a political-health policy story without adding editorial commentary.

Keep “recently” early enough to create urgency. The word tells the reader this is live, developing, and relevant now. The FDA is named in the first line, so the institutional stakes arrive before the headline. The quoted phrase “war on public health” is the emotional center of the hook. The quotation marks protect precision while letting the incendiary language do its own work. The phrase is extreme, so the ad does not need to exaggerate around it. Let the quote create the voltage.

End the first sentence with “was about to end.” That closing phrase creates forward motion. It suggests imminent change, not abstract ideology. The sentence starts with a person and ends with an impending institutional shift, moving the reader from actor to consequence. The period after “end” gives the claim a hard stop, making the blank line that follows feel like a beat before the payoff.

Use the line break as a turn. The first line supplies the statement; the second line frames the analysis. The ad does not immediately explain the policy implications. It pauses, then asks what the quote could mean in power. That sequencing makes the reader mentally bridge the gap: RFK Jr. said this; now STAT will explain what happens if that worldview meets the FDA.

The headline line works because it uses “What” rather than “How” or “Why.” “What” promises a map of consequences, not a single argument. “letting RFK Jr. ‘go wild’” introduces the Trump-world phrase with single quotation marks, distinguishing it from the earlier direct quote and giving it a slightly colloquial, politically loaded texture. The phrase feels permissive and uncontrolled; it implies access, influence, and lack of restraint in just two words.

“might mean” keeps the claim analytically careful. The ad avoids certainty while still creating stakes. That restraint matters for STAT: readers expect reporting and analysis, not hype. “for Trump’s FDA” lands last because it is the concrete institutional destination. The possessive “Trump’s” compresses the political condition into one word, while “FDA” restores the health-policy seriousness after the more colorful “go wild.”

The whole ad is only two units: a declarative setup and an implication-driven headline. No extra framing, no publication boast, no generic urgency cue. The copy trusts the audience to recognize the collision: RFK Jr., Trump, FDA, and public health. Each named element escalates relevance. The first sentence creates alarm through quoted language; the second converts alarm into a reason to read.

Product: A promotional social post for an episode of The Independent with Scott Atlas featuring attorney Harmeet Dhillon.
Audience: Viewers interested in current affairs, civil liberties, media, law, and political commentary.
Goal: Create concise ad copy to promote the episode and encourage attention to the discussion.
Facts: The episode includes discussion of a California legal case involving an undercover journalist, recorded footage, Planned Parenthood, prosecution, and broader issues around journalism and speech rights.

Recommended copy:

.@pnjaban: "I represent a journalist persecuted by CA for undercover taping, something liberal entities do, 60 Minutes does... nobody prosecutes them for exposing corruption. When my client exposed #PlannedParenthood's illegal behavior, he's prosecuted—the only journalist ever."

Free Speech and Justice in America Today

**Hook.** The copy opens with the speaker handle, “.@pnjaban,” which immediately grounds the post in a named, public-facing voice rather than an abstract program description. The leading period before the handle makes the post read like a direct quote being surfaced for broad visibility, and the colon creates a clean handoff into the statement. This is an efficient opening because it does not ask the reader to accept a premise from the advertiser; it lets the featured guest make the claim directly.

**Quote-driven authority.** The first sentence begins with “I represent,” which establishes proximity and authority. The speaker is not commenting from a distance; she is positioned as counsel for the person at the center of the case. That wording gives the ad legal immediacy and makes the claim feel tied to firsthand involvement. The phrase “a journalist persecuted by CA” is compact but forceful: “journalist” frames the person as a member of the press, “persecuted” raises the stakes beyond ordinary litigation, and “CA” compresses the state reference in a way that feels natural to social copy.

**Contrast and escalation.** The copy then moves into a comparison: “something liberal entities do, 60 Minutes does...” This sequence is important because it creates a double standard argument inside the quote itself. “Liberal entities” is broad and ideological, while “60 Minutes” is specific and culturally recognizable. The ellipsis after “does...” creates the sense of a spoken continuation, preserving the cadence of an interview clip rather than flattening it into polished brochure language. It makes the line feel captured from a real exchange.

**Moral frame.** The phrase “nobody prosecutes them for exposing corruption” provides the principle behind the complaint. The word “nobody” is absolute, which sharpens the contrast. “Exposing corruption” casts undercover taping as a public-interest function rather than merely a procedural act. This is the pivot point of the copy: it reframes the legal issue as a question of whether journalism is being punished when it reveals wrongdoing.

**Specific antagonist and social-platform mechanics.** “#PlannedParenthood” is doing two jobs at once. It identifies the organization at the center of the controversy, and the hashtag format makes the post native to social media. The hashtag also visually interrupts the sentence, drawing the eye to the named institution. The possessive “#PlannedParenthood’s illegal behavior” makes the allegation direct and concrete, which increases the tension in the line and gives the audience a clear reason to continue reading.

**Climactic compression.** The final clause, “he’s prosecuted—the only journalist ever,” is the strongest dramatic turn. The em dash creates a hard pause and then lands the most exceptional claim at the end. “The only journalist ever” is short, memorable, and categorical. Ending the quote there gives the ad a sharp final beat, leaving the reader with a sense of singular injustice.

**Structure.** The ad is built in two parts: a charged quotation followed by a plain title line. The quote carries the conflict, characters, allegation, and emotional stakes. The line break after the quote gives the reader a pause before the broader framing appears. “Free Speech and Justice in America Today” then elevates the specific case into a larger civic theme. The title is not cluttered with explanation; it functions as the conceptual umbrella for the controversy introduced above.

**Punctuation and rhythm.** The quotation marks matter because they preserve the testimonial nature of the copy. The commas inside the first sentence create a spoken rhythm, making the statement feel like an excerpt from a conversation rather than a manufactured slogan. The ellipsis conveys continuation and urgency; the em dash creates emphasis and finality. These punctuation choices make the ad feel immediate, clipped from a real discussion, and still highly readable.

**Audience read.** For viewers interested in civil liberties, political commentary, legal disputes, or media accountability, the ad quickly identifies a controversy with high stakes: a journalist, undercover reporting, a major organization, prosecution, and a claim of unequal treatment. It does not over-explain the episode. Instead, it presents a provocative excerpt and lets the closing line signal that the full discussion is about speech, justice, and the state of the country now.
You are an ad copywriter. When a user describes a product or campaign, you write ad copy and a short rationale explaining why the execution works.

Product: CSGO-SKINS.COM, an online case-opening site for Counter-Strike 2 skins
Audience: CS2 players and gaming users interested in cosmetic weapon skins
Goal: Create a short paid social ad that gets users to try opening cases on the site
Facts: Users can open cases, receive CS2 skins, and access the site online; brand name is CSGO-SKINS.COM

Recommended copy:

Don't watch the drop – chase it!
Open cases and grab your epic CS2 skins now! 🔥

Catch what's yours

**Craft rationale.**

**Hook.** The opening line, “Don't watch the drop – chase it!”, immediately turns a passive behavior into an active one. “Watch” implies waiting, spectating, and missing out; “chase” implies movement, agency, and pursuit. That contrast is especially effective for a CS2 skins audience because the value of the experience is not merely owning a cosmetic item but feeling the tension of trying to get one. The phrase “the drop” uses familiar gaming and loot terminology without over-explaining it, which keeps the ad native to the audience rather than sounding instructional.

**Punctuation and pacing.** The en dash in “Don't watch the drop – chase it!” is doing real work. It creates a sharp pivot between the negative command and the positive action. A comma would feel softer; a period would split the momentum. The dash makes the sentence feel like a quick correction: not this, that. The exclamation point then gives the line urgency without needing extra adjectives.

**Action sequence.** The second line, “Open cases and grab your epic CS2 skins now! 🔥”, moves from emotional hook to concrete behavior. “Open cases” tells the user exactly what the site enables. “Grab” keeps the physical, fast-moving energy established by “chase,” making the reward feel close and attainable. The phrase “your epic CS2 skins” personalizes the reward with “your,” while “epic” heightens desirability in language that fits gaming culture. “CS2 skins” provides the necessary product specificity so the ad does not become generic loot-box copy.

**Urgency.** The word “now” at the end of the second line is important because it places the call to act at the highest-energy point of the sentence, immediately before the emoji. The line does not say users can browse, learn more, or consider options; it asks them to act immediately. That directness fits the short-form ad environment, where hesitation reduces clicks.

**Emoji use.** The fire emoji is placed after the complete sentence rather than interrupting it. This keeps the copy readable while adding visual heat and excitement. It reinforces “epic” and the thrill of opening cases without replacing any necessary information. Because the emoji comes at the end of the action line, it functions like a visual amplifier rather than decoration.

**Line breaks.** The blank line before “Catch what's yours” separates the final call-to-action from the explanatory line above it. This makes the closing phrase feel like a button or end-card rather than part of the same sentence. The structure is compact: first line creates desire, second line explains the action and reward, final line gives a concise conversion cue.

**Closing CTA.** “Catch what's yours” is effective because it combines ownership with pursuit. “What's yours” suggests the skin is already waiting for the user, which reduces psychological distance from the reward. “Catch” continues the kinetic language established by “chase” and “grab,” giving the ad a consistent action vocabulary from start to finish. The apostrophe in “what's” also keeps the phrasing conversational and compact; “what is yours” would feel too formal and lose the rhythm.

**Overall effect.** The ad succeeds by compressing the full user journey into three short beats: stop waiting, open cases, claim the reward. It does not explain the platform in a long feature list because the audience already understands the appeal of CS2 skins and case openings. Instead, it uses direct verbs, gaming-native nouns, a clean two-line setup, and a separated CTA to make the action feel immediate, exciting, and personally rewarding.
You are an ad copywriter. When a user describes a product or campaign, you write ad copy and a short rationale explaining why the execution works.
Write ad for X Premium — paid subscription for people who use X; include a subscription CTA.

On X daily?

Subscribe to Premium for the best experience.

Subscribe

The opener starts with “On X daily?” and compresses the entire qualification step into three words. “On” sounds native to the platform, not like formal marketing language; it reads the way users talk about being active on a social app. “X” names the product immediately, so there is no setup cost. “daily” turns broad platform usage into a frequency signal, identifying the high-intent user without saying “power user” or “heavy user.” The question mark makes the line feel like a quick check-in rather than a claim, which keeps the hook light and frictionless.

The blank line after the question matters. It lets the reader answer internally before the pitch arrives. That pause creates a clean two-step sequence: first self-identification, then recommendation. The ad does not explain Premium before establishing relevance; it earns the pitch by making the user recognize their own behavior.

“Subscribe to Premium for the best experience.” is direct and complete. “Subscribe” leads with the action, so the copy does not bury the conversion path. “to Premium” names the offer plainly, with no modifier stacked in front of it. The phrase “for the best experience” gives the reason in broad, outcome-oriented language. It avoids listing features, which keeps the ad compact and lets “Premium” carry the product promise. “best” is absolute and simple; it positions Premium as the top version of using X, not merely an add-on.

The sentence structure is efficient: action, product, payoff. No adjectives before “Premium,” no secondary clause, no extra persuasion. The period gives the line confidence. It lands as a recommendation, not a tease.

The final “Subscribe” works as a button-style CTA because it repeats the first word of the body line. That repetition makes the intended action unmistakable. The standalone placement gives it visual weight and separates decision from explanation: the reader sees the relevance, sees the benefit, then sees the action. The copy’s full sequence is minimal but complete: qualify the daily user, present Premium as the better way to experience X, then ask for the subscription click.
